utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: next patient is melissa sanchez . date of birth , 9/23/1962 . mrn : 5484367 . she is being seen in office today for status post mitral valve repair done on 8/3/2020 . at her previous follow-up on 9/17 , we felt that she was doing quite well from a cardiac standpoint , and so we recommended she continue with the same medication . ecg taken on 12/26/2020 reveals atrial fibrillation with a controlled ventricular response . t-wave inversion anteriorly . compared to the previous study , there are no significant changes , and please add in the history , patient has a history of mitral regurgitation and atrial fibrillation , history of diabetes , asthma , and recurrent chest discomfort with negative cardiac workup for coronary artery disease .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: ms. sanchez , it's good to see you again .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , it's good seeing you too dr. hughes . you look like you're doing well .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i am , thank you . you too . you're looking great . how have you been feeling ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: pretty good . i'm definitely feeling better , thank goodness . i was having a rough time before surgery , um , but i think i'm overall pretty good now though .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . well , that's really good to hear . i'm glad . are you having any new symptoms right now ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , not really . but , you know , i'm still getting some chest pains sometimes , and my breathing gets shallow . but , i guess i'm learning what i can and ca n't do . uh , so if i feel like that , if i'm , like , exerting myself , i slow down a bit which helps , and then when i go back to it later , i can usually finish whatever i was doing .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so , it does go away though ? how long does an episode seem to last ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh , only a few minutes i guess . then it disappears for a while . it's weird .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: what is a while ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it goes away for a couple weeks . um , so when it happens , i just take the day nice and slow , i do n't push myself .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so , maybe it's a couple times a month you feel this way ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , just often enough for me to notice .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: understood . okay . are you taking your medications regularly ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: uh- . i've been trying to keep up with that . there's a lot of extra pills now , but i have a reminder app , so i do pretty well .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . that's great . and so , you're taking coumadin , right ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , also lasix and the atenolol .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: are you having any side effects from the medications at all ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not really . uh , i notice that the atenolol is making me irritable in the beginning , but i guess i've gotten used to it , does n't seem to bother me as much now .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . well , that sounds good . sounds like you're well on the mend . so , why do n't i start out , um , with the physical exam , and just check you out .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you're going to hear me describe things in detail or repeat things as i go to reference later for my notes .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay then . i'm going to be using my status post template , ms. sanchez , please lie down on the table here and we'll get started . all right . can you turn your head to the left . head and neck no jvd detected . you can turn back now and just take a couple of deep breaths for me please . okay , that's good . and lungs have reduced breath , but auscultation and percussion are clear . okay . breath normally , i'm just going to listen to your heart . rhythm is irregularly irregular .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's a funny statement . what does that mean ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , it does sounds kind of weird , right ? it's when your heart does n't beat with the correct rhythm , and whatever rhythm it does n't have a pattern to it . so , it's part of the atrial fibrillation .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: wow , that sounds like a mess .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah , it's not ideal . but , many people have a-fib are able to keep it under control with medicine and lifestyle changes .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: agh , i see . that's good to know .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay . so , s1 slightly accentuated , no s3 . i'm going to touch your belly , and does any of that hurt .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how about there ? and your feet ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , not really .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay , great . and trace peripheral edema on extremities . all right ms. sanchez , you can sit up now . so , it looks like your heart valves are working well and you are recovering from the surgery nicely .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: good .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes . we are , we looked at your ecg taken earlier today , and we are seeing the a-fib , but it's being well controlled with the medicine , and you're taking coumadin , four milligrams , lasix at four milligrams a day , and the atenolol , you're taking that every day as well , right ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes . the 50 milligrams every day .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great . it sounds like you're well on your way to recovery .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so , let's continue on your current meds . i'm glad that you're figuring out what your body can handle . definitely try to keep active as that will certainly help .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'll do my best .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and that's about it . do you have any questions for me ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , i do . when do i need to come back and get checked out ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well , i do n't think you need to come back soon . everything from a cardio perspective is fine . so , i think let's do a followup in about six to nine months .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right , thank you . it was good to see you again dr. hughes .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you as well ms. sanchez , do take care .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you too .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 58-year-old female with a history of mitral regurgitation, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, and asthma.
The patient is status post mitral valve repair from 08/03/2020.
An ECG from 12/26/2020 shows atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response and anterior t-wave inversion.
The doctor asks how the patient has been feeling.
The patient reports feeling better overall since her surgery.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing any new symptoms.
The patient reports intermittent chest pain and shallow breathing during exertion, which improves with rest.
The doctor asks about the duration of the chest pain and breathing episodes.
The patient states the episodes last a few minutes.
The patient states the episodes recur every couple of weeks.
The doctor asks if the patient is taking her medications regularly.
The patient uses a reminder app to maintain medication compliance.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing any medication side effects.
The patient reports initial irritability from atenolol, which has since subsided.
Physical exam findings: no JVD, lungs clear with reduced breath sounds, heart rhythm irregularly irregular with S1 accentuated and no S3, trace peripheral edema.
The patient is taking Coumadin 4 mg daily, Lasix 40 mg daily, and Atenolol 50 mg daily.
The doctor advises continuing current medications and maintaining physical activity.
The patient asks when she needs to return for a follow-up.
The doctor recommends a follow-up visit in 6 to 9 months.

case_description: follow up on mitral valve repair; none
